display_name: High CPU on BGP process after topology change is expected, not a bug
context: Observing high CPU utilization on the BGP process after a link failure, peer reset, or large route withdrawal/advertisement
observation: BGP process (bgpd, bgp_speaker) shows 80-100% CPU utilization after a topology event
naive_interpretation: The BGP process is stuck or has a bug causing excessive CPU consumption
correct_interpretation: After a topology change, BGP must recalculate best paths, run the decision process, update the RIB/FIB, and send updates to all affected peers. This is compute-intensive and expected to spike CPU temporarily. The question is not whether CPU is high, but whether it returns to normal within a reasonable convergence window.
why: BGP best-path calculation is O(n) per prefix where n is the number of paths — with a full internet table this is millions of comparisons
Route updates must be generated per peer group with appropriate policy application
FIB updates via the RIB-to-hardware programming pipeline add additional processing load
This is the routing protocol doing exactly what it is designed to do

confirmation_commands.purpose: Check if BGP has finished converging — if convergence is complete and CPU is still high, then investigate further
Check peer count and total prefixes to gauge expected convergence time

confirmation_commands.command: show processes cpu sorted | include BGP
confirmation_commands.purpose: Observe CPU trend for the BGP process — take multiple samples to see if it is decreasing

traps: Opening a TAC case for high BGP CPU that is actually normal post-convergence processing
Restarting the BGP process to fix CPU that would have resolved on its own, causing a second convergence event
Not differentiating between transient post-event CPU spike and sustained high CPU indicating a genuine problem
Using a single CPU sample rather than trending over 2-5 minutes to determine if the spike is resolving
